chunk_summary: Why organic social media marketing is the best early growth channel for apps: fast feedback, low cost (free except time), more creative freedom than Reddit, and faster results than SEO. Paid ads require a large budget to test properly.
content: This video is going to be the ultimate social media marketing guide for developers trying to market and grow their apps on the internet through social media. ... I've been building apps for the past five or six years. I built over 14 different apps. ... all of the marketing that I did to grow my apps and make money from them ... was strictly through organic social media marketing. ... And we've built multiple apps that have grown to roughly 2 grand a month of revenue. And currently, the app that we're building, which is called Yori, it is currently hovering at around 6 or 7 or 8K a month of revenue, depending on the month. ... There's a particular reason why I really love social media marketing, especially in the early stages of the app development process. Number one is I think it is the fastest way to get feedback about your app and marketing in general. ... With organic social media marketing, this is really fast response times, cheap, basically free aside from the time that you spend to actually make the videos, but in the beginning time is cheap, time is free. ... Paid ads is fine, but then it's really, really, really expensive. ... you probably need to allocate roughly $1 to $2,000 to really get a thorough test of a paid ads to see how it works. ... Reddit marketing is also pretty good. But my biggest con with Reddit is that less creative freedom ... you're restricted to making a post strictly only via text. ... SEO takes a really long time. It is a slow burn process. ... in the early stages when I think you're really just trying to get some quick validation of what works and whether your idea works or does not, I still think organic social media marketing is the best solution for it.
questions_on_this_chunk: Why is organic social media marketing better than paid ads for early-stage apps?
What are the advantages of social media marketing over Reddit or SEO?
How much budget do you need to test paid ads effectively?
What results has this speaker achieved using social media marketing?

chunk_summary: How the social media algorithm works: it pushes your video to an initial group, measures watch time and engagement, then expands to similar audiences if it performs well. The cycle repeats until the audience stops engaging.
content: So, let's talk about how does the algorithm work. ... you post a video into the internet. ... the algo then pushes your video out to a bunch of initial viewers and in those initial viewers and then the algorithm determines how does this initial group of people, how are they viewing the app? Are they liking it? Are they watching it for a long time? Basically, that is the biggest metric that you have to optimize for watch time. How long are the users staying and watching that video? ... And then let's say it does well with this initial cohort of people. Then the algo is like, 'Hey, this video seems to be doing pretty well with this cohort of people from this original group of people. Let's find people similar to them and then send it out to more and more people like all these people.' And then it keeps sending out to a greater and greater group of people until eventually the algorithm detects that it has reached a group of people that are no longer really watching the video for all that long. ... Then the video dies out. That's really all it is. Super super dead simple.
questions_on_this_chunk: How does the TikTok or Instagram algorithm decide which videos to promote?
What is the most important metric for the social media algorithm?
How do followers affect initial video distribution?

chunk_summary: Account warm-up process: before posting, use the account like a real user for 3-5 days — scroll, engage, and follow accounts in your niche. This prevents being flagged as spam and getting zero views.
content: Number one is you need to warm up your account. So, these businesses of Meta, YouTube, Tik Tok, they have entire departments dedicated to fraud and spam and bot detection. It is not a great signal if you just make account and start posting like crazy. They're going to be like, 'Whoa, whoa, whoa. A high chance that this is a spam account.' So you need to convince them ... you need to show them prove to these platforms that you are a real person using this account for real purposes. So during this process scroll around I would say probably the first 3 four 5 days or so scroll around like a real user. A couple minutes every day maybe multiple times a day like 10 15 minutes scrolling sessions for three to four to five times a day. Do that for three to four or five days. And also make sure to follow and engage with accounts and content that is in a similar niche as to where you are trying to market your app ... so that the algorithm is kind of trained on what you like and try to attract that group of people into your feed. ... Trust me, you will know if your account is kind of viewed as spam or like a bot. If you post a video and you realize it gets like 10 views or five views or worst case zero views, you've kind of been shadowbanned because they detected that you're a bot. And in that case, delete the account and make a new one.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do I warm up a new social media account before posting?
What happens if I post without warming up the account?
How can I tell if my account is shadowbanned?

chunk_summary: The hook is the most important part of any short-form video. Spend 80-90% of effort on the first 2-3 seconds to capture attention. Great hooks prey on curiosity and pose a problem that the rest of the video solves. Example hooks and the 'hook and demo' format are shown.
content: The most important part of making any type of short form content is that you need to hook the users in. You need to convince the users to watch your video. It doesn't matter how incredibly good your video is in the last 95% of the video if that first 5% isn't good enough to convince the users to stay in tune and watch the rest of it. So really when making your videos, you should probably spend 80 to 90% of your efforts on that first two to three seconds. What's your opening line? What's your opening hook? What's your opening visual to convince the users to stay and watch your video all the way through to the end? ... the more of your video that is watched in terms of raw time, raw duration, the higher chance that your video is going to be pushed out into the algorithm. That's what you need to optimize for. ... I'm going to show you this account right here. This is an account that we legitimately run and use to market our app, Yorbi. ... Let's see, this got 120K views. Let's see what the hook is. 'RIP to all creators using chat GPT in 2026.' Wo, that is a that's a spicy hook, right? It kind of feeds on this innate sense of human curiosity ... we present this hook as a problem. And once we got the user hooked in, we kind of have them for the rest of the video. We have earned the right to show them the rest of our video. And in this scenario, we just show a quick product demo of how exactly your work works. posed the problem in the hook and then we provided the solution afterwards immediately ... That's the general anatomy of how we constructed this hook.
questions_on_this_chunk: What makes a good hook for a short-form video?
How much of the video creation effort should go into the first few seconds?
Can you give an example of a hook that uses curiosity effectively?

chunk_summary: Popular content formats for SaaS marketing: 1) Hook and demo — pose a problem, then demonstrate the product as the solution. 2) Talking head — talk directly to camera and weave in the product naturally. 3) Skits — high viral potential but lower conversion. 4) Carousels — static images with a hook, great for AI-generated content and high engagement.
content: Now, let's talk about some popular formats, video formats that you can use to market your app. So, I showed you one earlier, which is the Autumn Luna format, right? We call this one the hook and demo cuz I mean kind of simple. We show a hook with a juicy hook and then we solve that hook. We satisfy the user's itching and craving that we gave them in the hook with a demo of the product. ... This is great. It is the best format out there probably if you can get views cuz it clearly plugs your product and you can get a lot of views. Another format is talking head videos. ... For people that are really good at talking and just have really good pros in general, talking head formats are also really good because you can have this long drawn out story where you go into details here and there and then you can plug in your product. ... Another popular format that can get really viral and get a lot of views but doesn't necessarily convert as well for users signing up and paying customers are skits. ... even though these videos ... got way less views ... these converted way, way higher than any of these skit videos. ... Don't worry too much about going really mega viral. Your main concern should just be going viral amongst the right group of people. ... Another really popular format and really, really effective format is the carousel format. ... within Instagram and Tik Tok, rather than just posting videos ... you can just post a bunch of photos and slide through them like a photo carousel. ... I think even Tik Tok came out explicitly and said carousels get like 5x the amount of engagement as videos. ... you need a juicy hook as your first slide. But now the benefit of this is that you don't need real videos in the background. You can just have static images. And AI can generate static images way better than it can generate moving dynamic videos. So, if you're trying to do AI content, you should definitely into the whole carousel method.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the hook and demo content format?
When should I use talking head videos instead of skits?
Why are carousels effective for social media marketing?
Which format is best for converting viewers into customers?

chunk_summary: Posting strategy: post at least once a day, but no more than twice to avoid burnout and low-quality content. Treat it like a venture capital play — most content will flop, but the 1% that hits will drive growth. Copy what works from competitors and when you find a winning format, double down on it until it saturates.
content: I think you need to post minimum once a day. And yes, this helps just to, you know, get your more content out into the algorithm because essentially the whole organic social media marketing game is essentially it's a VC play. You're going to have like a venture capital investment play. 99% of the content that you make is going to flop, but that 1% that's going to break out and go crazy. That's going to drive a lot of growth for your app. You just need to get a lot of shots on goal. ... I would not post too much content. I would honestly put the ceiling at like two pieces of content a day cuz I think any more than that, you're going to run the risk of burning out and you're also going to run the risk of just producing really low trash content. ... Also, when you're doing this, don't reinvent the wheel. Don't feel like you have to create a brand new video format. Truly, just copy what works, what your competitors are doing, what's going viral at the moment, and that will make your life so so much easier. ... And when you do find something that does work, it is up to you to just double down on it like crazy. ... we spammed the living hell out of this format. ... once we found out that it worked, we invested so much time and energy to just pumping out so much of this content and doubling down on it like crazy. So that's really the game of marketing. Try out a hundred different things, see whatever does work out of those hundred and double down, triple down on it, and keep rinsing and repeating until that run, that format, that marketing strategy runs out of juice. Go back to the drawing board, try out a 100 different things again, rinse and repeat.
questions_on_this_chunk: How often should I post on social media for my app?
Why is it okay if most of my videos flop?
Should I copy my competitors' content formats?
What should I do when I find a content format that works?

chunk_summary: Brand accounts vs. founder-led marketing: dedicated brand accounts (like Autumn Luna) are more efficient for pure marketing because they can post unlimited ads without diluting a personal brand. Founder-led marketing helps but is less explosive. Best strategy: create multiple dedicated accounts for your app.
content: Now let's get into some of the nuances of like brand accounts versus like personal accounts. So a brand account is something like this. Like this is an account that is strictly dedicated for the sole purpose of marketing Yorby, our app that we're building. ... it's honestly kind of spammy. We're going over and over again talking about the same video, the same format ... and doesn't provide much value outside of being just a pure marketing and advertising engine for our app. And I get a lot of comments ... saying, 'Bro, like it's easy for you to build an app because you have a huge audience.' ... you'd be surprised with how much less having your existing audience helps for the most amount of people. ... Yes, having your own personal account does help for marketing purposes. ... But I'm telling you right now, the most effective way of marketing is definitely spinning up completely dedicated accounts like the Autumn Luna account, this one, strictly to market your app because it is just so dedicated strictly to marketing. It will have so much more return on investment than having your own personal brand, personal account. ... I just think that this type of marketing can be a lot more explosive compared to trying to build your own brand. ... in every single app that I've personally ever built solo or apps that I've built with my co-founder Andrew, we always create multiple dedicated accounts strictly to market that app. That's the best strategy. You can only do so much with your own like personal account, personal brand without like diluting your own personal brand and personal account. Whereas when you make an account or a page strictly dedicated to marketing your app, there's no type of dilution because there's literally you can post an unlimited number of times and that's okay because that's the whole sole purpose of that account. So you should create multiple accounts and it's okay to have multiple accounts.
questions_on_this_chunk: Should I use my personal account or create a separate brand account for my app?
What are the advantages of a dedicated marketing account?
Is founder-led marketing less effective than anonymous brand accounts?
How many social media accounts should I create for my SaaS?

chunk_summary: AI vs human content: algorithms don't care if content is AI or human, only if it's good. AI content works best when the creator doesn't talk (carousels, reaction-style videos). Talking-head AI content degrades quality. Human creators are better for talking-head and personality-driven formats.
content: Now the next thing I want to talk about is AI versus human content. ... this whole account like this is an AI generated account. this person is not real. Like this is all AI image gen, AI video gen and it still works. But we also have hired real human creators to make content for us as well. So then it's like wait what gives? At the end of the day it doesn't matter. The algorithms don't care if your content is human created or AI created. All they care about is is the content good cuz now especially like the lines are so blurred about what's AI versus human that instead they're just like dude if the content's good and people want to watch it people are going to watch it. ... I will say though with AI generated content, at least we've kind of seen, they typically do better on formats where they're not really talking. ... I think AI image gen like static image generation is way better than video generation. But video generation is also pretty good these days as long as they're not talking. The minute you get them to sync their lips with speaking I think the quality degrades a little bit and more people are able to tell that that it's AI ... So that's why with a carousel type of format or this type of format, a hook and demo, where the AI creator is not even speaking, I think this is the most effective format for using AI content, just content where they're not speaking and they're just still image or videos of them with a facial reaction. Basically, don't talk. I think talking kind of ruins it ... I think if you're going to go down the AI route, you cannot do talking head. I don't think it works nearly as well.
questions_on_this_chunk: Can I use AI-generated content for social media marketing?
What type of AI content performs best on social media?
Should AI-generated characters talk in videos?
Do algorithms penalize AI content?

chunk_summary: Where to find UGC creators: search on TikTok/Instagram for accounts with <10K followers in your niche who are charismatic. Also use marketplaces like SideShift (large but unvetted) and UGC Tank (vetted but more expensive). Look for creators who have gone viral before or have >5K followers on a personal brand.
content: In this part of the video, I'm going to be talking about where to find and hire UGC creators and generally the best practices when it comes to interviewing them and managing them. So, there are a couple places to look and find for UGC creators. You can find them directly on the platforms like Tik Tok and Instagram. Basically, what you would do is you'll go to Tik Tok or something and you go into search bar and generally I'll search up something that's already within your niche. ... And I'll literally just go to videos and I'll start scrolling down the page. ... And what I'm looking for is someone that a is charismatic on camera. I think can represent my brand and also isn't too big yet. Cuz when they're too big of a following, they probably aren't really interested to make content for your brand exclusively because they're making enough money from brand deals already. So, someone like this person, he's talking on camera, he seems very charismatic. He's someone that I think would be a good fit. ... I'm genuinely looking for someone that has like less than 10,000 followers. ... But anyways creator marketplaces there are two main ones that I recommend. Side shift this is a marketplace that is probably the biggest and the most popular to my knowledge for UGC content at least. ... The downside of it is they let anyone sign up to a platform. ... Another platform you can use is UGC Tank. So this is a platform where every single creator on this platform is vetted. ... I personally have used both. I'm currently using UGC Tank, but I think it's really up to you which one you prefer. ... Criteria to look for. So, two things. Number one, has made content for another SAS app or has a personal brand with over 5,000 followers. And if they make content for another SAS app ... Make sure they also have gone viral. So, they understand what it takes to go viral for a brand ...
questions_on_this_chunk: Where can I find UGC creators for my app?
What criteria should I use to select a UGC creator?
What are the best UGC creator marketplaces?
How do I approach a creator I found on TikTok?

chunk_summary: How to pay UGC creators: use a base rate ($25-$35 per video) plus a bonus tier based on views (e.g., $40 for 10K views, $500 for 500K views). Alternatively, pay a CPM of $1-$2 per 1,000 views. The speaker currently prefers per-video bonus structure. Ensure the creator's earning potential aligns with performance.
content: How much to pay creators? So, there's two ways to pay creators. One is what you see on screen right now, which is a bonus tier payout, which basically means you pay them a base rate of generally $25 to $35 per video. and then based on the monthly views. You can do monthly views on the whole account or you can do views based on individual videos that are being created. And actually, let me show you that right now. Here's an example of a payment structure you can give out to creators if you're basing them off of individual videos that are posting. ... they post a video, they get the $25 base rate, and within a two-week window or four-week window, generally people do two weeks. If the video hits 10K views, they get $40. If it hits half a million views, they get $500 for that video. If they hit 4 million views, they get 1,400. These don't add up. It's just like within that two weeks, if they hit any of these, you know, view impression metrics, they get these type of payouts. This is one method. ... Another method I've seen people do is CPM payment. So, it's basically just strictly tied to like how many impressions these creators are generating. ... I've seen anyone pay creators from $1 to $2 CPMs. And usually they also start tapering off the CPMs like 50 cents, 10 cents if it go past like a million impressions, 2 million impressions, 3 million impressions, etc. ... The current structure I'm running is this one where we pay creators based off of individual video performance ...
questions_on_this_chunk: What is a typical payment structure for UGC creators?
How much should I pay a UGC creator per video?
What is CPM and how is it used for creator payments?
Should I pay creators based on individual video views or total monthly views?

chunk_summary: Interviewing and managing creators: on the call, assess if they can do the job (past viral content) and want to do it. Avoid creators with too many other commitments. Give them example videos to imitate; creators are often lazy. Set clear benchmarks: 1K views by 5 videos, 10K by 10 videos, 100K by 20 videos. If they miss, cut them quickly.
content: Okay, interviewing the creator. When you get on a call with the creator, and I genuinely recommend you guys to hop on a call with the creator, especially when you're starting out and you don't understand exactly what you're looking for. I'll try to get two things out of them, right? The main purpose. Number one, can they do the job? And number two, do they want to do the job? ... I'll check if they have time to do the role. What other commitments do you currently have? This is roughly a five hour per week commitment. Look, if they're working like two jobs, they have kids and they have like all these other things going on. That's usually a red flag for me. ... managing creators. So, generally, you know, I like to give creators a lot of creative freedom to make what they want. But I will also just show them example videos you wanted to imitate or make. ... The reason I do this is because creators are lazy. again assume that like no one's going to care as much about your brand as much as you care about your brand. ... If they don't hit a viral video by the first month, aka 100K views, I would just cut them. Generally, I have rarely seen a creator that hasn't gone viral in the first month like turn around eventually. ... My benchmarks are five videos in, have a video hit 1,000 views. 10 videos in, have a video hit 10K views, and 20 videos in, you should have a video to hit 100K views. If you don't, either creators bad, like the content format's bad, or the creator is just not making good content for you, or you don't have product market fit ... or your product just not good fit for social media ...
questions_on_this_chunk: How do I interview a UGC creator?
What are red flags when hiring a content creator?
What performance benchmarks should I set for a new creator?
When should I fire a creator who isn't getting views?

chunk_summary: Final rapid-fire tips: don't be afraid to create and delete multiple accounts; hashtags don't matter much; treat each account like a TV show with consistent format; balance between pure plug content and value-only posts. Two strategies: all marketing spam (higher short-term) or mix of value and marketing (longer shelf life).
content: All right, thanks so much for Andrew for talking about how to hire and manage creators and then kind of concluding and wrapping up this video. We're going to go through some like rapid fire points. Number one, do not be afraid to make more accounts. ... Dude, just make a new account. It's not that deep. Make a lot of them. Delete them. Make new ones. Delete them again. That's the name of the game. ... Point number two is hashtags don't matter. Sounds rarely matter. So don't overthink too much about that. Really the star of the show, the thing that matters the most is the true content of the video, specifically the hook and then the rest of the video. ... Lastly, treat each account like it's their own TV show. try to keep the format pretty similar and so that users if they follow that account, they know what to expect. ... Another rapidfire point I want to make is ... how much content should you post on your account that is always a product plug plugging your product versus making content that is also not plugging your product. ... When you make an account that is just purely spamming the algorithm with videos to market and advertise your product, that has a higher immediate short-term potential to get views and users and signups. But the long-term potential is significantly lower because ... 'This account's boring. I don't want to watch it anymore.' ... Whereas the alternative strategy is you can make an account like let's say you post five times a week. Three of those posts can be just post about the general niche ... and then you can make two videos that week actually talk about your product and plug that product. ... it has a longer shelf life and can live longer with more just general fans and followers. ... Honestly, best case scenario is you make an account of each type.
questions_on_this_chunk: Should I make multiple social media accounts for one app?
Do hashtags help with reach?
How should I balance promotional content vs. value content?
What does it mean to treat a social media account like a TV show?
